title: pas_admin::register_pas_asset does not check is_pas_coin / is_coin_registered early
description: register_pas_asset<T> neither asserts !treasury.is_pas_coin<T>() nor !treasury.is_coin_registered<T>() before calling pas::policy::new_for_currency. A second registration attempt (or registration of a T already registered as a regulated coin via admin::register_coin) will abort either deep inside pas::policy::new_for_currency at EPolicyAlreadyExists, or inside add_pas_coin_caps at ECoinAlreadyRegistered. The surface abort happens after global PAS state has partially changed (policy derivation attempt). While atomicity of the PTB means nothing is committed, the error is not local and is harder to diagnose.
impact: Opaque failure when the caller misconfigures registration; poor DX and harder debugging of failed partner integrations.
recommendation: At the top of register_pas_asset, add: assert!(!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered); (or a dedicated EPasAlreadyRegistered) before invoking any PAS upstream fn.

title: register_pas_asset does not call version.check_is_valid()
description: register_pas_asset takes `version: &Version` but never invokes `version.check_is_valid()`. The safety argument is that `auth.assert_is_valid_for_treasury` exists and the caller had to mint a `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` which internally calls `version.check_is_valid()` (see auth::new_treasury_auth). However, since TreasuryAuth has `drop`, a previously-built auth could hypothetically be reused within the same PTB across modules, and the defensive pattern in the rest of this codebase is to always re-check version when the module is entered. Lack of re-check here makes the function rely on upstream invariants of auth.move and weakens the emergency-pause semantics.
impact: If version is blocked mid-PTB (theoretical) or if future refactors of auth drop the version check, register_pas_asset would not fail fast. Weakens defense-in-depth for emergency upgrades.
recommendation: Add `version.check_is_valid();` as the very first statement in `register_pas_asset` and `update_transfer_template`, matching the pattern in `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` (which does check).

title: pas_supply::mint does not call version.check_is_valid()
description: pas_supply::mint never calls `version.check_is_valid()`. It relies on the version check inside `auth::new_auth` when `Auth<MintPermission, T>` was minted. Since `Auth` has `drop`, re-use in the same PTB is not a direct threat, but the pattern is inconsistent with pas_transfer::approve_transfer which explicitly re-checks version. If auth construction were ever refactored or if a future non-auth path was added, mint would silently bypass version gating. The same concern applies to burn and seize, neither of which receives a Version.
impact: Weak defense-in-depth for emergency pause / version-block. Not critical because auth construction currently checks version.
recommendation: Either (a) pass `version: &Version` to mint/burn/seize and check at the top, or (b) document the reliance on auth-side version check explicitly in a module-level doc comment.

title: pas_transfer::approve_transfer is permissionless but reuses mint/redeem pause flag
description: approve_transfer is invoked as part of PAS autoresolve and is effectively permissionless — anyone with a valid Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>> can call it. It gates on `!is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()`. This conflates three distinct pause concepts: (1) pause mint/redeem only, (2) pause transfers only, (3) emergency halt. The current code uses a single flag for all, so a compliance officer who only wants to stop new issuance (but keep transfers flowing to allow existing holders to exit) cannot do so. Conversely, a temporary transfer freeze would also block mint/redeem. This is a design decision, not a bug, but it should be documented clearly and considered against the permission model.
impact: Operational inflexibility. Compliance officers lose granularity for partial-pause scenarios. Existing holders may be unable to exit when supply is paused for regulatory reasons.
recommendation: Either add a separate `TransfersPausedKey<T>` + `PauseTransfersPermission`, or document that pausing mint/redeem also freezes PAS transfers by design. Mention explicitly in the module-level comment of pas_transfer.

title: WhitelistManagePermission accepts any registered coin T, including non-PAS coins
description: `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>` is built via `auth::new_auth<P, T>`, which only asserts `treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()`, not `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()`. A coin registered via `admin::register_coin` (regulated, not PAS) would satisfy is_coin_registered, allowing an address with WhitelistManagePermission (on paper for PAS) to mint an `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, RegulatedCoin>` and call `add_to_whitelist`. This then aborts deep inside `dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut` at `treasury::add_to_whitelist` because the whitelist DOF was never created for that coin — opaque abort rather than a clean error.
impact: Opaque aborts on misuse; no explicit check that the coin is actually a PAS coin when managing whitelist. Adversary cannot subvert compliance, but DX is poor and the guard is a latent DOF-borrow error.
recommendation: Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin);` at the top of each whitelist mutator in pas_admin (or — cleaner — in the treasury::add_to_whitelist / remove / set_send / set_receive functions).

title: No explicit test suite for any new PAS module
description: There are no `#[test]` functions in `pas_admin.move`, `pas_supply.move`, or `pas_transfer.move`, and the repo has no `tests/` directory. None of the new state transitions (register_pas_asset, add_to_whitelist / remove_from_whitelist, set_whitelist_send/receive, mint/burn/seize, approve_transfer) are exercised by automated tests. Given that PAS integration touches cross-package invariants (policy / account / templates / requests) and is the entire value of this PR, this is a significant gap. Compare with upstream PAS which ships `pas/packages/pas/tests` coverage.
impact: Refactors and upstream PAS upgrades will silently break invariants. Review-only verification is insufficient for the blast radius (mint, burn, seize, transfer approval).
recommendation: Add unit tests for: (a) register_pas_asset happy path + double-registration rejection; (b) whitelist add/remove/set_send/set_receive + idempotency/error cases; (c) mint to non-whitelisted account rejected; (d) approve_transfer happy path + rejections for non-whitelisted sender/recipient + zero amount + paused; (e) seize to non-whitelisted destination rejected; (f) burn path with real Request; (g) policy cap lookup for non-PAS coin returns ENotPasCoin.

title: Move.toml pins pas/ptb deps to branch `main`, not a commit SHA
description: Both pas and ptb git dependencies are pinned to `rev = "main"` (branch name) instead of a commit SHA. The build lockfile pins `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`, but the manifest itself is not reproducible from source: anyone regenerating the lockfile against a newer `main` will silently pull different upstream semantics. Upstream PAS behavior changes (e.g., additions to required_approvals action strings, account.owner semantics, send_funds::resolve_balance destination) would be absorbed without notice.
impact: Non-reproducible builds; silent upstream drift can break security invariants (e.g., if upstream changes `account.deposit_balance` target or clawback semantics). Makes CI / audit remediation traceability fragile.
recommendation: Replace `rev = "main"` with the audited commit SHA (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc` currently in Move.lock). Document the dep-bump process so updating these deps is an explicit, reviewed change.

title: Move.toml edition is `2024.beta` — prefer stable `2024`
description: The main package Move.toml declares `edition = "2024.beta"`. The stable edition is `2024` (already used in token_template_pas/Move.toml). Mixing `2024.beta` and `2024` across packages in the same workspace is inconsistent. Move 2024 has been stable since early 2025 per the sui-pilot docs; beta edition may be dropped or diverge.
impact: Potential future compiler incompatibility and inconsistency between the main package and its helper package.
recommendation: Change `edition = "2024.beta"` to `edition = "2024"` in Move.toml to match token_template_pas/Move.toml.
evidence:
[package]
name = "hadron"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024.beta"

title: WhitelistManagePermission collapses separate add/remove permissions (spec drift)
description: The Notion spec defines `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission` separately; this PR introduces a single `WhitelistManagePermission` covering both add and remove (and set_send/set_receive). This is a valid simplification but drifts from the specification and reduces the granularity operators can use to model a separation-of-duties role (e.g., onboarder-only vs. offboarder-only).
impact: Operational: institutions cannot separate the permission to add addresses from the permission to remove them, which is a common regulatory pattern.
recommendation: Either (a) split back into `WhitelistAddPermission` + `WhitelistRemovePermission` (+ optionally `WhitelistUpdatePermission`) to match spec, or (b) update the design spec in Notion to reflect the single-permission decision and flag in release notes.
evidence:
// ============ PAS Whitelist ============

public struct WhitelistManagePermission {}

title: pas_supply::mint event emits account.owner(), not the account object address
description: pas_supply::mint calls `account.deposit_balance(balance)` which, per upstream `account::deposit_balance`, forwards the balance to `object::id(account).to_address()` — i.e. the PAS account object address, not the wallet owner. However, the emitted `PasMinted` event records `account.owner()` as `recipient`. Off-chain indexers inspecting event-only data will see the owner address, not where the balance actually lives. For regulatory reporting, both should be exposed (or only the account object address if that's the point-of-truth).
impact: Off-chain reconciliation ambiguity: the event's `recipient` is the human-visible owner, but the on-chain balance sits at the derived account ID. Reconciling via Sui balance queries by address will not match events unless indexers know the deposit semantics.
recommendation: Add `account_id: ID` to `PasMinted` (and `PasBurned`, `PasSeized` to and from) so indexers can link events to account objects without needing upstream PAS knowledge. Alternatively, document this in pas_supply.move.

title: pas_transfer::approve_transfer validates wallet sender/recipient, but balance goes to recipient_account_id
description: approve_transfer checks `treasury.assert_can_send<T>(sender)` and `assert_can_receive<T>(recipient)` using the wallet addresses from `SendFunds::sender()` and `::recipient()`. Per upstream `send_funds.move` L69-70 (`/Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/requests/send_funds.move`), resolve_balance sends funds to `recipient_account_id.to_address()`, NOT the wallet address. This is consistent with upstream PAS intent (wallet address = logical identity; account_id = storage address), but means the whitelist is a wallet-level whitelist and a de-whitelisted wallet could still have its PAS account receive funds if the caller bypasses the approval. Because the autoresolve template always invokes approve_transfer, this is safe in the happy path — but if Templates is ever modified, a direct SendFunds construction could bypass the whitelist.
impact: Relies on PAS Templates + required_approvals chain to enforce whitelist; a malicious namespace-admin-like attack or a future upstream refactor could split these guarantees.
recommendation: Document explicitly in pas_transfer.move that the whitelist is wallet-scoped and enforced via PAS required_approvals + template autoresolve. Consider also validating `sender_account_id` / `recipient_account_id` belong to the same namespace (defense-in-depth).

title: register_transfer_template uses TransferApproval<T>'s address_string — stale on package upgrade
description: `register_transfer_template` resolves `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>()` and uses `address_string()` for the package_id in the ptb::move_call command. This binds the template to the **defining package address** at registration time. After a package upgrade, the defining address stays the same (defining IDs are rooted at v1), so the template will continue to dispatch to v1's pas_transfer::approve_transfer. That is likely desired (stable autoresolve binding), but it also means an upgraded pas_transfer logic will NOT be picked up until `update_transfer_template` is called AND — critically — the address_string continues to be the v1 address. Any upgrade to pas_transfer logic is therefore dead-code unless update_transfer_template is invoked after upgrade. Additionally, the template path is not guarded by is_pas_coin.
impact: Package upgrades to pas_transfer approval logic require a manual follow-up call; easy to forget. Stale templates could leave an institution running old validation logic in perpetuity.
recommendation: Emit an event from register_transfer_template (e.g. PasTemplateRegistered<T> { package_id }) so indexers can track template staleness. Document the upgrade runbook in pas_admin.move.

title: update_transfer_template does not assert is_pas_coin<T>()
description: update_transfer_template<T> takes `&Treasury` (immutable) and re-registers the template for type T without asserting that T is a PAS coin in this treasury. A caller with RegisterCoinPermission could invoke update_transfer_template<SomeRandomT> and it would still succeed (it only writes to the Templates registry, keyed by TransferApproval<T>). This is unlikely to be harmful because Templates is a PAS-level registry and the worst outcome is a stale template entry for a type that doesn't exist on-chain, but it's silent misconfiguration.
impact: Operators can accidentally register templates for non-PAS types, polluting the Templates registry. No security impact because downstream validation still happens in approve_transfer.
recommendation: Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin);` at the top of update_transfer_template.

title: WhitelistEntry exposes no event on default add (can_send / can_receive both true)
description: `add_to_whitelist` inserts `WhitelistEntry { can_send: true, can_receive: true }`, and `pas_admin::add_to_whitelist` emits `WhitelistAdded<T> { addr }`. The event does NOT carry the initial send/receive booleans. Consumers watching only add/remove events cannot reconstruct the full whitelist state without also watching `WhitelistSendUpdated` / `WhitelistReceiveUpdated`. Because the default is `(true, true)`, current-state reconstruction is possible, but if the default is ever changed to `(false, false)` or a future change lets callers pass custom defaults, event consumers will silently miss state.
impact: Weak event observability; fragile if defaults change in a future revision.
recommendation: Either (a) extend `WhitelistAdded<T>` to include can_send, can_receive fields, or (b) guarantee in a doc comment that default is (true, true) and add a reminder to update the event if the default changes.

title: treasury::is_whitelisted / can_send / can_receive abort for non-PAS coins
description: `is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, and `can_receive` immediately call `dynamic_object_field::borrow<...>` on the whitelist DOF without first checking `is_pas_coin<T>()`. For a non-PAS coin T, the whitelist DOF does not exist, so these three read-only predicates abort rather than returning `false`. This breaks the expected read-side contract that boolean predicates are total. Off-chain callers / other modules cannot safely call `can_send` to compose behavior.
impact: Predicates intended as safe read queries instead abort; breaks composability and requires every caller to check is_pas_coin first.
recommendation: Guard with is_pas_coin: `if (!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()) { return false }` at the top of is_whitelisted / can_send / can_receive. Keep the asserting variants (`assert_can_send`, `assert_can_receive`) as the abort path.

title: supply::mint / redeem PAS guard is post-condition to cap acquisition check order
description: In both `supply::mint` and `supply::redeem`, the sequence is: `amount > 0` → `is_pas_coin` → `assert_mint_redeem_enabled` → cap borrow. The is_pas_coin check is fine, but note: an already-registered regulated coin cannot become a PAS coin (registration paths are mutually exclusive by the treasury_cap DOF uniqueness check). So the practical effect of the EPasCoinNotAllowed guard is to hard-fail when someone calls supply::mint with a T whose policy_cap DOF was set via add_pas_coin_caps. Correct. The minor quality concern is naming: the guard phrases PAS registration as a "not allowed" error rather than clarifying the dispatch rule. Consider a `#[deprecated]` / redirect suggestion in the error. (Already does in the error message — acceptable.)
impact: None from a correctness standpoint. Pure readability suggestion.
recommendation: No change required. Optionally move the `is_pas_coin` check before `amount > 0` so that generic-type validation happens before input validation (more common convention).

title: scripts/src/operations/pas.ts: pkg() may return empty string if deploy-output.json missing
description: Every PAS operation builds `package: pkg()` and type arguments like `${pkg()}::permissions::RegisterCoinPermission`. `pkg()` in scripts/src/constants.ts returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`, both of which default to `''` if deploy-output.json is missing. A call chain invoking `registerPasAsset(...)` without prior `bun run deploy` will silently construct a transaction with `::permissions::RegisterCoinPermission` (empty package) and an invalid target — the error only surfaces at SDK submission with a confusing message. There is no pre-check.
impact: Confusing error for first-time operators and automation; a missing deploy output results in a deep SDK parse error instead of a clear actionable message.
recommendation: In `pkg()` or at the top of each exported operation (registerPasAsset, addToWhitelist, removeFromWhitelist, mintPasTokens, deployAndRegisterPasAsset), throw an explicit error when `pkg()` returns empty: `if (!pkg()) throw new Error('Package ID not set; run `bun run deploy` first');`.

title: scripts/operations/pas.ts: amount: number for u64 — precision loss at >2^53
description: `mintPasTokens(... amount: number ...)` and the internal `amount,` argument serialization rely on JS `number` for what is a u64 on-chain. Any amount above Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (2^53-1 = 9_007_199_254_740_991) will silently lose precision on the JS side. For a 6-decimal token, the safe limit is ~9.007 billion whole tokens — reachable for institutional issuance. The @mysten/sui SDK accepts bigint for u64, so the right contract is `amount: bigint`.
impact: Silent amount truncation at scale; high-precision institutional mints can end up with the wrong on-chain amount without errors.
recommendation: Change the function signature to `amount: bigint` and pass it directly; remove the implicit conversion. Same change for examples/full-setup.ts call sites (`mintPasTokens(..., 1_000_000)` becomes `mintPasTokens(..., 1_000_000n)`).

title: deployPasTokenTemplate: hard-coded bytecode without regeneration script
description: The PAS token template bytecode is hard-coded as a single hex string with no regeneration script, no comment tying it to a source commit, and no test that recompiles `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` and asserts byte-equality. The companion Move source exists at token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move but there is no CI check that the hex matches the current source. If token_template.move is edited (new decimals default, new struct fields, new framework deps), the hex will drift silently and deployments will publish stale bytecode.
impact: Silent drift: the published PAS token template may not match the source-tracked Move module. Partners reading the source will see one behavior, the network will run another.
recommendation: Add a `pnpm build:bytecode` script that builds token_template_pas and emits the hex into getBytecode.ts. Add a CI step that rebuilds and `diff`s against the checked-in file. Document the dep list (0x1, 0x2) as a constant with a comment.

title: deployPasTokenTemplate: missing 0xA / 0xC dependencies at publish time
description: The publish dependencies list in deployPasTokenTemplate.ts is `[0x1, 0x2]`. The Move source uses `sui::coin_registry::{Self, MetadataCap}` and `sui::coin::TreasuryCap`. coin_registry lives at 0x2 (sui framework) in current sui framework, so the listed deps may be sufficient. However, many template examples include 0xa (accumulator) / 0xc (coin_registry object ID, not package ID — this is where confusion lies). Verifying this once via a devnet publish dry-run is necessary.
impact: If a newer sui framework splits coin_registry out of 0x2 or requires additional published modules as deps, the publish call will abort with a dependency-missing error at runtime.
recommendation: Confirm the full dependency list by running a dry-run publish against the target network in CI, or explicitly enumerate the deps by walking the bytecode's module handles and including each unique address.

title: deployAndRegisterPasAsset extracts coinType via string substring match — fragile
description: The script identifies the TreasuryCap, MetadataCap, and Currency objects with `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')` and `t.includes('::coin_registry::MetadataCap<')`. This matches by substring on fully-qualified type names. If sui framework ever renames modules or introduces a differently-named inner type, the match will silently fail and the post-condition `if (!treasuryCapObjectId || ...) throw` triggers. Beyond brittleness: the script constructs coinType by splitting symbol with `.replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, "_")` and assuming module name is `symbol.toLowerCase()`. If the Move module name is NOT `symbol.toLowerCase()` (e.g., custom module_name in TokenTemplateValues), the coin type is wrong and downstream calls fail opaquely.
impact: Fragile ID extraction; module-name mismatch produces wrong coinType and wrong generics, causing all subsequent operations to abort with type-mismatch errors.
recommendation: Instead of inferring, use `obj.objectType` from the transaction response and parse the `<inner>` type parameter with a regex like `/::coin::TreasuryCap<(.+)>$/`. Store the full coinType after extraction. Same for MetadataCap. Don't construct coinType from symbol.

title: token_template_pas Move.toml has no explicit edition or deps
description: token_template_pas/Move.toml declares `edition = "2024"` (good) but has an empty `[dependencies]` section. Since Sui 1.45+ the MoveStdlib and Sui framework deps are implicit, so this is technically correct. Consider adding a comment clarifying this for reviewers unfamiliar with the implicit-deps convention, since the main hadron Move.toml explicitly lists external deps.
impact: Documentation / readability only.
recommendation: Optional: add a comment `# MoveStdlib and Sui framework are implicit in Sui 1.45+` inside the `[dependencies]` block.
evidence:
[package]
name = "token_template_pas"
edition = "2024"

[dependencies]

title: pas_supply::seize allows seizing zero-balance-remaining state ambiguity (no source-was-whitelisted check)
description: Per design (spec §7.2), `seize` intentionally does not assert `assert_can_send<T>(from)` — seize is how a regulator extracts funds from a de-whitelisted address. It does require `assert_can_receive<T>(to.owner())`. Correct and documented. However, there is no event or assertion that the source address is actually deregistered from or present in the whitelist — so seize can be applied to an arbitrary (non-whitelisted, never-whitelisted, or currently-whitelisted) address indiscriminately. This gives a SeizePermission holder the ability to clawback funds from any PAS account, not only from flagged / de-whitelisted addresses.
impact: Designed behavior, but regulatorily sensitive: seize is unrestricted; a compromised SeizePermission holder can move funds to any whitelisted receiver.
recommendation: Document explicitly in the module header that SeizePermission is unrestricted with respect to source. Consider emitting which policy-driven justification the seize is operating under (e.g., a bytes32 reason code in PasSeized), for audit trails.

title: Duplicated error constant EMintRedeemPaused across treasury and pas_transfer
description: pas_transfer defines its own `EMintRedeemPaused` error constant duplicating the one already defined in treasury (`EMintRedeemDisabled`). Worse, the two use different names (`Paused` vs `Disabled`) for what appears to be the same concept. This creates two error codes for logically-the-same failure condition, depending on where the check fires. Off-chain consumers decoding abort codes will see different error strings for "mint/redeem paused/disabled", which complicates monitoring and error-handling UX.
impact: Inconsistent error surface; harder to categorize aborts; two strings for the same underlying state.
recommendation: Either (a) route pas_transfer's pause check through treasury::assert_mint_redeem_enabled to share the same error (`EMintRedeemDisabled`) and drop `EMintRedeemPaused` from pas_transfer; or (b) unify by exporting the error from treasury and re-using it. Option (a) is preferred.

title: pas_admin events: no event emitted from register_pas_asset for template registration
description: `register_transfer_template` writes to the Templates registry (shared state) but emits no event. `update_transfer_template` (which re-registers after an upgrade) also emits no event. This means indexers cannot observe template registration / re-registration on-chain. The only PAS event for registration is `PasAssetRegistered<T>` emitted from treasury via `add_pas_coin_caps`, which confirms cap storage but not template setup. For operators tracking upgrade consistency this is opaque.
impact: No on-chain evidence of template registration; upgrade compliance / staleness detection requires reading Templates DOF directly.
recommendation: Add an event `PasTemplateRegistered<T> { package_id: address }` emitted by `register_transfer_template`. This also helps detect when an older package version is still templated (R2-012).

title: full-setup.ts example combines regulated and PAS flows — risk of cross-contaminated state
description: The full-setup example creates a single treasury and then registers BOTH a regulated coin (HUSD via deployAndRegisterCoin) and a PAS coin (HBOND via deployAndRegisterPasAsset). This is probably intended as documentation that a single treasury can hold both coin kinds, which is good. However, the example omits any negative-path demonstration (e.g., "what happens if you try supply::mint on a PAS coin"). An onboarding engineer may not realize the guards are there.
impact: DX / documentation. Not a correctness issue in the example itself.
recommendation: Add a commented-out block in full-setup.ts that demonstrates the expected failure: "// Attempting supply::mint on a PAS coin will abort with EPasCoinNotAllowed". Or split into two example files.

title: pas_admin::register_pas_asset passes &mut treasury_cap to policy::new_for_currency but keeps ownership
description: `policy::new_for_currency` takes `_cap: &mut TreasuryCap<C>` — per upstream `/Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/policy.move` L46-50, the cap is an unused witness (`_cap`). Hadron's caller correctly passes `&mut treasury_cap` and retains ownership, then later transfers ownership to `add_pas_coin_caps`. Correct. However, the upstream signature uses `&mut` but the argument is prefixed `_cap` (unused). This is a flaky upstream API contract — if upstream ever narrows this to `&TreasuryCap<C>` (reasonable, since it's unused), Hadron would not need `mut` on the local binding. Low-priority stylistic concern.
impact: None. Stylistic / future-compatibility note.
recommendation: Optional: add a comment noting why the local binding is `mut treasury_cap` (required by upstream's `&mut` even though upstream ignores the cap).

title: No check that pas_namespace.policy_exists<Balance<T>>() matches treasury state
description: Upstream `policy::new_for_currency` asserts `!namespace.policy_exists<Balance<C>>()` (at policy.move L51). In Hadron's design, a single Namespace is global and shared across all treasuries, so a policy created by one institution's Treasury blocks any other Treasury from registering the same coin type T as a PAS asset. This is a single-tenant-per-coin constraint imposed by upstream PAS. Hadron does not document this anywhere; two treasuries attempting to register the same coinType T as PAS will see the second one abort at upstream EPolicyAlreadyExists. Since coin types T are 1:1 with OTWs (unique per package), this is unlikely in practice but possible if someone tries to reuse a T across treasuries.
impact: Cross-institution coin-type collision blocks second registration; opaque upstream abort. Practically rare but surprising.
recommendation: Document in pas_admin module comment: "The PAS Namespace is singleton; a coin type T can only be a PAS asset for a single institution. Registering the same T in two treasuries aborts the second with EPolicyAlreadyExists."

title: scripts/constants.ts: PermissionTypes adds whitelistManage / seize but no test that they match Move
description: `PermissionTypes` enumerates all permission type fully-qualified names as strings. Adding `whitelistManage` and `seize` requires typing them in sync with the Move source. There is no codegen-enforced linkage; a typo or stale permission type will produce a parse error only at TX submission. The rest of the codebase uses sui-ts-codegen (see package.json `codegen` script), but PermissionTypes is hand-maintained.
impact: Future Move refactor that renames a permission leaves `PermissionTypes` out-of-sync; silent drift caught only at runtime.
recommendation: Generate PermissionTypes from Move via the existing codegen pipeline, or add a runtime sanity check at startup that verifies each permission struct exists at the target package.

title: PasBurned event loses symmetry: no amount-prior-to-burn or resulting balance
description: `PasBurned<T> { from: address, amount: u64 }` records only the amount burned. For audit trails and reconciliation, it's useful to know the account's balance before/after. `from` here is the wallet owner (from `request.data().owner()`), not the account ID. Again, like R2-010, indexers cannot distinguish which account was burned from without walking the Account objects.
impact: Weaker audit trail; regulator / compliance indexers need extra reads to reconcile.
recommendation: Add `account_id: ID` to PasBurned (and PasSeized) so the event is self-contained. Same concern as R2-010.
